html:
Paul Justen, also known as Paulus Juusten, born around 1516 in the village of Juustila in the parish of Viborg, died on August 22, 1575, in Turku, was a Finnish bishop during the Swedish era.

== Biography == Juusten studied at the University of Wittenberg from 1543 to 1546 and served as rector of the cathedral school in Turku from 1548 to 1554. In the latter year, he was appointed the first bishop of the Diocese of Viborg, and a decade later, he was transferred to the bishopric of Turku, where he served as bishop from 1563 to 1575. In 1569, Juusten was sent by King John III as a Swedish envoy to Tsar Ivan in Moscow, whose displeasure the Swedish envoys inadvertently incurred. Juusten was subjected to mistreatment and imprisoned, where he remained for three years. Finally released, he returned to Finland with failing health and died in his hometown of Turku. In 1574, he translated Luther's Small Catechism into Finnish. During his time in Russia, he compiled an analysis of the year's sermon texts for the Finnish clergy, which remained unpublished. Professor Henrik Gabriel Porthan arranged for the publication of his account of his mission to Russia, "Acta legationis moscoviticæ," and his chronicle of the bishops of Finland, "Chronicon episcoporum Finlandensium." The bishop was ennobled in 1572 by King John III. He was one of the bishops who, at the king's command and with due observance of Catholic ceremonies, consecrated Laurentius Petri Gothus as Archbishop.

== See also == Bishop of Viborg List of Bishops of the Diocese of Turku

== References == Juusten, Paul in Herman Hofberg, Svenskt biografiskt handlexikon (second edition, 1906)

=== Notes ===

== Further Reading ==

  • Juusten, Paul; Kajanto, Iiro (1995). *The tragic mission of bishop Paul Juusten to tsar Ivan the terrible: the itinerary of the delegation to Moscow.* Annales Academiae scientiarum Fennicae. Ser. B, 0066-2011 ; 276. Helsinki: Suomalainen tiedeakatemia. Libris 7833482. ISBN 951-41-0760-8
  • "Juusten, Paul." *Biographical Dictionary of Finland.* Helsinki: Svenska litteratursällskapet i Finland. 2008–2011. URN:NBN:fi:sls-4463-1416928957069
  • Gillingstam, Hans: Paul Juusten in the *Swedish Biographical Dictionary* (1973–1975)

How certain can we be about this?

From the Vasa era onwards, individuals are well documented in archives. The uncertainty lies more in interpretation and how posterity views them – especially since those in power themselves played a role in shaping historical narratives.
tldr: Paul Juusten was a Finnish bishop who became a prisoner in Russia for three years after being sent there as an envoy by the Swedish king.
lattlast:
Paul Juusten was born around 1516 and worked as a bishop, a high-ranking position within the church. He served as bishop in both Viborg and later in Åbo. During his time as leader, he wrote down stories about his experiences and translated religious texts into Finnish.

In 1569, he was sent by King John III of Sweden to Russia to meet with the Tsar, which was the title held by Russian rulers at that time. There, he was treated poorly and imprisoned for three years. Upon returning to Finland, his health was severely compromised, and he died shortly thereafter.

Before his death, he was ennobled by the king, meaning he received a prestigious title signifying high status. He is remembered for his writings about his journey to Russia and for the texts he translated for the Finnish clergy.
